name: Shimōsa
description: A prosperous province centred on the Tone River. Its fertile plains yield abundant rice, while its coast provides fish and salt. The Chiba clan holds sway here from their castle at Sakura.

name: Kogane
description: A bustling river port on the Tone River, the beating heart of Shimōsa’s trade. Merchant houses line the canals, and boats laden with rice and salt depart daily for Edo and beyond.

name: Sakura
description: An ancient castle town perched on a wooded hill. The Chiba clan’s fortress overlooks the surrounding plains, and samurai patrol the streets. A small market serves the garrison and local farmers.

name: Chōshi
description: A fishing village at the mouth of the Tone River. The air smells of brine and drying fish. Small boats dot the harbour, and a lighthouse guides ships around the dangerous cape.

name: Narita
description: A quiet village centred around a Shingon temple. Pilgrims pass through on their way to the great shrine, and the monks keep a peaceful garden that is said to heal the soul.

name: Yachimata
description: A small farming hamlet surrounded by vast millet fields. The land is flat and fertile, and the farmers are sturdy, hard‑working folk who rarely see outsiders.

name: Kogane Woods
description: Dense mixed forest of oak, maple, and pine. Deer and wild boar roam the undergrowth, and woodcutters’ paths criss‑cross the shadows.

name: Tone Riverside
description: The broad Tone River flows steadily here. Its banks are thick with reeds, and herons stand motionless in the shallows. A good place for fishing or launching a small boat.

name: Shimōsa Coast
description: Sandy beaches stretch along the eastern sea. Fishing boats dot the horizon, and at low tide women and children gather shellfish among the rocks.

name: Hitachi
description: A rugged province of rocky coasts, deep forests, and iron‑rich mountains. The Satake clan rules from the formidable castle of Mito, and the province’s iron sands fuel its smithies.

name: Mito
description: The seat of the Satake clan. A formidable castle overlooks the eastern sea, its stone walls rising sheer from the cliffs. The city hums with the activity of samurai, merchants, and artisans.

name: Kasama
description: A market town in the hills, known for its stone quarries and an ancient Inari shrine. Farmers bring their produce here, and the clink of the stonemason’s hammer is never far away.

name: Hitachiōta
description: A remote mountain village. Charcoal burners and hunters make their living here, and the forest is said to be haunted by a white stag that grants wishes to the pure of heart.

name: Nakaminato
description: A small fishing port with a natural harbour. The catch is dried and sent inland, and the villagers tell tales of sea monsters that dwell beyond the horizon.

name: Hitachi Forest
description: Deep woods of ancient cedar and cypress. The forest floor is dim and quiet, broken only by the occasional deer trail. Woodcutters fear the tengu that are said to dwell in the oldest trees.

name: Hitachi Mountains
description: Rugged peaks with sparse vegetation. Iron ore veins are visible in places, and the wind howls through the passes. Only the hardiest yamabushi venture here in winter.

name: Hitachi Coast
description: Rocky shoreline with crashing waves. A dangerous but beautiful stretch where shipwrecks are common, and the survivors speak of sea spirits dragging vessels onto the rocks.

name: Shimotsuke
description: A land of rolling hills and broad plains, famous for its horses. The Utsunomiya clan controls the province from their castle, and the great Nikkō mountains rise in the north.

name: Utsunomiya
description: The provincial seat, famous for its horse markets and the Utsunomiya clan’s hilltop castle. The streets are wide and often muddy, filled with the snort of horses and the call of traders.

name: Nikkō
description: A sacred mountain village at the foot of the holy peaks. The forests here are said to be home to tengu, and the shrines are among the oldest in the land. Pilgrims come from far away to pray.

name: Mooka
description: An agricultural village on the fertile plain. Barley and soybeans grow in abundance, and the local sake is prized for its smooth taste.

name: Utsunomiya Highlands
description: Rolling hills covered in susuki grass. Wild horses can sometimes be spotted grazing, and the view from the highest ridge takes in the entire plain.

name: Nikkō Mountains
description: Steep, sacred peaks. Waterfalls plunge into moss‑covered gorges, and ancient shrines hide among the crags. The air is cold and pure, and one can walk for days without seeing another soul.

name: Kōzuke
description: A province of wide plains and high mountains, contested by the Uesugi and the remnants of the Nitta clan. The Tone River flows through it, bringing trade and strife in equal measure.

name: Takasaki
description: A trade hub on the Usui River, where merchants from the mountains meet those from the plain. The castle here has changed hands more times than anyone can count, and the people are wary of strangers.

name: Maebashi
description: A growing town on the Tone River. Rice paddies stretch to the horizon, and the river port is busy with boats carrying grain to Edo. The Uesugi have a strong garrison here.

name: Numata
description: A remote highland village perched on a mountain pass. The people are hardy and distrustful of outsiders, but they are loyal to those who earn their respect. The pass is snowed in for half the year.

name: Tone Riverbank
description: The broad river winds through the plain, its waters cold and swift. Fishermen cast their nets here, and children swim in the shallows in summer.

name: Mikuni Mountains
description: The pass to Echigo. Snow lingers here even in spring, and the trail is treacherous. Bandits are said to lurk in the ravines, preying on unwary travellers.

name: Musashi
description: The vast Kantō plain's heartland. Musashi is a province of sprawling rice paddies, winding rivers, and strategic coastal access. Edo, a growing port town, lies on its southern shore, attracting settlers and merchants from across the land.

name: Edo
description: A bustling port town on the Sumida River. Its strategic location and fertile surroundings are drawing more and more settlers. The fish market is renowned, and rumours say the Shogun himself has taken an interest in its growth.

name: Kawagoe
description: A castle town built on the great plain, known for its sturdy fortifications. The Kawagoe clan watches the roads for the Shogun, and the town's warehouses are always stocked with rice and salt from the surrounding villages.

name: Hachiōji
description: Nestled in the foothills, this village is a stopping point for travelers heading west toward Kai. The surrounding forests are home to charcoal burners and a small Shinto shrine dedicated to the mountain kami.

name: Arakawa
description: A fishing and farming village along the Arakawa River. Ferries cross here, and the inn by the riverbank serves simple meals to weary travellers.

name: Musashi Plain
description: The vast Kantō plain stretches endlessly, covered in tall grasses and wildflowers. Farmers burn fields in spring to enrich the soil, and mounted samurai patrol the roads.

name: Sumida River
description: A wide, slow river teeming with fish. Herons stand motionless in the shallows, and small boats ferry goods between Edo and the inland villages.

name: Musashi Coast
description: The shore of Edo Bay, where shellfish gatherers work the tidal flats at dawn. On clear days, Mount Fuji can be seen to the west, a perfect white cone against the sky.

name: Kazusa
description: A coastal province of gentle hills and sheltered coves, famed for its shellfish and the great shrine at Ichinomiya. The warm currents bring abundant marine life, and the forests are home to skilled charcoal burners.

name: Ichinomiya
description: A prosperous coastal town known for its shellfish and the great shrine to Awa-no-kami. Pilgrims from across the Kantō come here to pray for bountiful harvests and safe voyages.

name: Mobara
description: An inland village surrounded by wooded hills. The soil is good for orchards, and the villagers are known for their sweet plums and persimmons.

name: Katsuura
description: A deep‑water fishing port where bonito and tuna are brought in daily. The town is famous for its fish market, and the smell of grilling seafood fills the streets.

name: Kazusa Hills
description: Gentle hills covered in bamboo groves and scattered farmsteads. Wild boar are common here, and a wise traveller keeps a sharp eye on the undergrowth.

name: Kazusa Coast
description: Dramatic cliffs and small coves. Sea hawks nest in the rocks, and the best shellfish are found in the tidal pools at low tide.

name: Awa
description: The tip of the Bōsō Peninsula, where the mountains meet the open ocean. The currents are treacherous, and the people are a hardy, sea‑faring folk who worship the great shrine at Tateyama.

name: Tateyama
description: The peninsula's end, dominated by the great sea shrine. Pilgrims and fishermen alike seek the protection of the kami who dwells here. The waters are rough, and the cliffs sheer.

name: Shirahama
description: A white‑sand beach village where fishermen brave the open ocean. The sunsets here are said to be the most beautiful in the eight provinces.

name: Awa Cliffs
description: Sheer cliffs drop to churning waters. The view stretches across the sea to distant islands, and the cries of gulls echo against the rock face.

name: Awa Mountains
description: Forested peaks at the peninsula's spine. Isolated and wild, the trails are overgrown and the silence is broken only by birdsong and the rustle of boar.

name: Sagami
description: A province of deep history and natural beauty. Kamakura, the ancient shogunal capital, still holds the Great Buddha and countless temples. The coast is indented with bays, and Mount Fuji watches over it from the west.

name: Kamakura
description: The ancient seat of the Minamoto shogunate. Temples and warrior houses cling to the wooded hills, and the Great Buddha sits in silent meditation. The streets are steeped in history, and a quiet elegance pervades the city.

name: Odawara
description: A strategic castle town guarding the Tōkaidō road. The sea laps at its walls, and the castle's towers command a view of the entire bay. A stopping point for all who travel between the capital and the eastern provinces.

name: Hakone
description: A mountain village near the great lake. The pass to the east is steep but well‑travelled, and hot springs steam among the volcanic rocks. Travellers often rest here before the final push to Kamakura.

name: Sagami Bay
description: The blue bay stretches wide. Fuji can be seen on clear days, its perfect cone reflected in the water. Fishermen cast their nets here, and the bay is said to be the home of a benevolent dragon king.

name: Hakone Mountains
description: Volcanic peaks and hot springs. The air smells of sulfur and cedar. The old Tōkaidō road winds through the pass, and at the highest point stands a small shrine to the spirits of travellers.
